title: Fun Quiz or Data Mining?
introduction: This post is going viral. Tap anything suspicious, then press Submit.

script.text:
OMG you HAVE to try this!! 🦸 Find Your SUPERHERO NAME:

1. Your mother's maiden name = Your hero first name
2. The street you grew up on = Your hero last name

Mine is Clarke Morrison! Share yours in the comments! 😂
script.explanation: 'Mother's maiden name' and 'street you grew up on' are two of the most common bank security questions. This quiz is harvesting exactly those answers — from thousands of people who think they're just having fun. The shareable format ensures wide distribution.

script.text: ANOTHER VIRAL POST: 'What decade were you born in? Your birth year + your pet's name = your rockstar name! Mine is 1987-Biscuit. Comment yours!'
script.explanation: Birth year plus pet name. Combine this with your mother's maiden name from the previous quiz and an attacker has three common security question answers and your approximate age — enough to attempt account recovery on banking and email services.

script.text: ANOTHER POST: 'Tag your best friend and they have to answer too!'
THIRD POST: 'Tag 5 friends and answer these to find out what city you should live in! Favourite childhood teacher? First car? Primary school name?'
script.flag_label: More security questions disguised as fun
script.explanation: Childhood teacher, first car, and primary school are all standard security questions used by banks, email providers, and government services. Each innocent-looking question adds another piece to a profile being assembled without the user's knowledge.

script.text: These all look like fun — I'll do all three and tag my friends.
FOURTH POST: 'Share your results and see what your friends get!'
[Months later: several of the user's accounts have been accessed. The attacker used the publicly posted security question answers to bypass account recovery on email and banking services.]

learning_objectives: Viral quizzes asking for personal details are often security question harvesting
Mother's maiden name, first street, first pet, first car are standard security questions
Posting these answers publicly makes your accounts vulnerable
Change security questions to random answers you store in a password manager
If you've posted these publicly, update your security questions now
debrief:
This type of data harvesting is extremely effective because victims never know it happened. The 'quiz' collects answers from thousands of people simultaneously. Combine multiple quizzes and you build a complete security question profile for each person.

**The fix is simple:** Stop answering these quizzes publicly. And more importantly — change your account security questions to random nonsense answers stored in a password manager. 'Mother's maiden name' should be 'purple-helicopter-44', not your actual grandmother's surname.

**If you've already posted:** Your answers are public and searchable. Update the security questions on your email, banking, and social accounts immediately.
